title: Fitting a thermostatic radiator valve
subtitle: Drain down, swap the lockshield end, fit the TRV head
excerpt: How to replace a manual radiator valve with a thermostatic radiator valve (TRV): isolating the radiator, draining it down, removing the old valve body, fitting the new TRV body and head, and refilling the system.
sourceNotes: Heating and Hot Water Industry Council (HHIC) guidance on TRV fitting (publicly available). Collins Complete DIY Manual. BS EN 215 specification for thermostatic radiator valves.

content.text: A
TRV
replaces the manual on/off valve at the flow-pipe side of a radiator. The
lockshield valve

on the return side does not need to be changed. The job requires draining the radiator down (not the whole system) and making two pipe connections. TRVs are sold as a body and head; some manufacturers also require a specific adapter nut to fit the radiator tapping.
Step 1, isolate the supply, drain the section, catch the residual

Close the existing manual valve fully (clockwise to close). Close the lockshield valve on the return side by removing the plastic cap and turning clockwise with an adjustable spanner. Count and record the number of turns for the lockshield (typically one to three turns); this setting controls the radiator's flow balance and must be restored when refitting. Place a bowl and large cloth under the flow-side valve. Crack the manual valve cap-nut by a quarter-turn; water will drain from the radiator down into the bowl.
Removing the old valve
Step 2, remove the old valve body.

Once the radiator has drained (hold the bowl under the cap-nut as you loosen it fully), unscrew the two nuts that hold the valve body: the larger one connecting the valve body to the radiator tapping (the angled nut at the top) and the cap-nut connecting the valve to the supply pipe. Remove the old valve body.
Fitting the TRV
Step 3, clean the tappings.
Clean the radiator tapping and the supply pipe with emery cloth. Remove any old PTFE residue from the threads. Check the pipe is undamaged.
Step 4, fit the new valve body.

Apply PTFE tape (5 wraps, clockwise) to the male thread of the new TRV body that connects to the radiator tapping. Fit the valve body to the radiator tapping and tighten with the adjustable spanner. Connect the cap-nut to the supply pipe; this joint has a copper olive and does not require PTFE tape. Tighten firmly.
Step 5, fit the TRV head.
Screw or clip the thermostatic head onto the valve body per the manufacturer's instructions. Most heads click onto a standard 30 mm nut; some require a specific adapter.
Step 6, refill and test.

Restore the lockshield valve to its original setting (the same number of turns from closed that you recorded). Open the TRV to maximum. Slowly open the main system pressure (or restore the central heating water pressure if needed). Bleed the radiator of any air. Check all joints for drips.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Close both valves, drain the radiator again, remove the valve body, apply fresh PTFE tape, and re-tighten.
attrs.cause: PTFE tape not applied, or the valve body was not tightened sufficiently.

attrs.fix: Turn the TRV head to the highest number setting. Check the lockshield is open to the correct original balance setting.
attrs.cause: TRV head is in the closed (low-number or frost-setting) position, or the lockshield was not returned to the original setting.
attrs.symptom: Radiator does not get hot after TRV fitting
